# The Problem(s) with Professional Development for Teachers

06/06/25
|
by
[Alyssa Meyers](https://moreland.edu/resources/author/alyssa-meyers)

Share

One of my worst days as a teacher was a professional development day. My district had a PD quota for teachers, and if you didn’t hit a certain number of hours during the school year, you had to make up the difference after school let out.

One year, I was 8 hours behind, so I had to sit there for a full 8-hour day after the last day of school. For a teacher who knows she’s supposed to be on summer break, that’s just about the worst feeling in the world.

You can guess how much I learned that day.

Don’t get me wrong, I’m a huge fan of teacher professional development — *when it actually develops the teacher*. But too often, like with my forced summer-school PD, it misses the mark.

Below are **8 common problems with teacher professional development** and how to fix them to ensure PD is useful, fresh, inspiring, and applicable in your teachers’ day-to-day lives.

## Problem #1: PD Feels Like Extra Work

Teaching is such a demanding job. After lesson planning, grading, making resources, running clubs, and fielding phone calls and emails from parents (not to mention the actual time spent, you know, teaching), there’s little time left for anything “extra.” Even the best professional development can feel like a punishment if teachers aren’t given space in their schedule.

**Solution**

When you add professional development to a teacher’s workload, try to balance it by taking something else off their plate. Perhaps you could cover lunch or recess duty for a teacher to free up time for PD. Or, instead of holding PD before or after school, schedule it during an in-service day or swap out a less-productive staff meeting with a more meaningful development session.

## Problem #2: Superficial Topics

Sometimes the topics introduced during PD sessions sound promising but fail to go beyond surface-level information. I’ve left more than one PD session thinking, “I already knew all of this. I could have spent that time grading.” If teachers aren’t learning new strategies or concepts, these sessions become a waste of time.

**Solution**

PD planners should focus on depth rather than breadth. Try combining several smaller sessions into one deep-dive workshop that offers actionable takeaways. Teachers should leave with tangible strategies, tools, or lesson ideas they can implement in their classrooms the very next day.

## Problem #3: Mismatched PD Assignments

One of the most frustrating PD challenges is being forced to attend sessions that don’t align with your role or needs. As a Spanish teacher, my classroom culture tended to greatly differ from core-subject classrooms, and I often left PD sessions feeling frustrated and like I didn’t need to be there. Similarly, an experienced teacher might be required to sit through entry-level training and leave the session feeling disappointed that there was nothing new for them.

**Solution**

Ask your teachers! What do they want and need from professional development? What are their interests, challenges, and growth areas?  Tailoring sessions based on teacher feedback ensures your PD will be relevant, empowering, and valued by staff. Some of the best PD days my school offered were ones where the teachers could choose what sessions to attend, moving from one classroom to another as the day went on. Those mini sessions were offered on a wide array of topics and designed to be relevant for different teachers at different stages of their careers.

![](https://moreland.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/7-ideas-infographic-ad-2-1024x512.jpg)

## Problem #4: Lack of Interactive Learning

Have you ever sat through a PD session on how to make learning more student-centered, only for the presenter to stand at the front of the room and lecture for hours? The irony isn’t lost on teachers who know firsthand that meaningful learning requires engagement and interaction. Most people learn best through hands-on experience, and that doesn’t just go for our students!

**Solution**

PD sessions should mirror classroom best practices. Just because teachers are grownups doesn’t mean they don’t love playing games to learn! Use hands-on activities, group discussions, role-playing, and other participatory strategies to ensure teachers are actively involved and applying what they learn.

## Problem #5: Over-Saturation

Too much of a good thing can quickly become a bad thing. When schools bombard teachers with frequent PD sessions, it can breed frustration and resentment. If every few days there’s another workshop, webinar, or online training, teachers might start saying: “This could’ve been an email.”

**Solution**

Rethink quantity and spacing in your professional development. If you zoom in on high-quality, targeted, and impactful sessions, less is often more. Plus, you’re freeing up your teachers’ calendars to give them a better work-life balance, which, in turn, reduces their stress and makes them better teachers for their students.

## Problem #6: More Theory than Reality

Sometimes PD can feel disconnected from the reality of teaching. Topics might be overly theoretical or designed more for optics than practical use. Have you ever left a PD session saying about the presenter, “You can tell they were never a teacher…”? For teachers juggling real-world challenges every single day, sessions like these can seem tone-deaf.

**Solution**

Hire practitioners with deep classroom experience to lead PD sessions. They’ll understand what works in real schools and real classrooms, making their guidance more valuable. Schools should also encourage teachers to bring examples of the challenges they’re facing to PD sessions to ensure the strategies being taught align directly with their day-to-day experiences.

![](https://moreland.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/partner-with-moreland-ad-1024x511.jpg)

## Problem #7: Lack of Follow-Up

Even the best PD sessions can fall short if there’s no follow-up. Often, teachers leave a great workshop excited to implement what they’ve learned — only to be left without ongoing guidance or resources. There were too many PD sessions I left saying, “That’s a great idea, but *how* do I do it?”

**Solution**

Professional development shouldn’t end when the session does. Schools can offer follow-up meetings, coaching sessions, or peer mentoring opportunities to help ensure sustainability and effectiveness. (Just make sure these extra meetings and sessions aren’t recreating Problem #1!) If you can’t offer these follow-up meetings, then do your best to avoid PD that ends on a cliffhanger. Teachers don’t want to be left in suspense!

## Problem #8: Lack of Collaboration

Perhaps the most underrated problem with PD is the missed opportunity to build community and collaboration among teachers. Many sessions involve sitting passively in the same room as coworkers, but with no actual interaction. Especially for those who teach in schools as big as mine was (both in population and in physical size), these PD sessions might be one of the only times you ever get to see some of your fellow colleagues!

**Solution**

Facilitate networking during PD sessions. Incorporate group activities, team-building exercises, and brainstorming discussions to encourage teachers to connect and share insights. A collaborative culture benefits both educators and students in the long run.

## A Better Future for Teacher Professional Development

Professional development can and should be transformational, providing teachers with practical tools to enhance their classrooms and careers. However, doing this requires a shift in how sessions are planned and delivered. By addressing these 8 common problems, schools can ensure every session challenges and inspires educators, rather than draining their time and energy.

Want more resources for school leaders? Join our monthly [Shaping Education newsletter community](https://moreland.edu/shaping-education-newsletter) for actionable ideas, inspiring stories, and helpful resources to share with your staff.

FULL CONTENT:
# Challenges facing teacher development in a school’s environment

Teacher development is a cornerstone of educational progress, contributing to the growth and success of both educators and students.

![Nartey Ephraim](https://res-3.cloudinary.com/adiutor-co/image/upload/q_auto/v1/ghost-blog-images/profile_image_cropped.jpg)

#### [Nartey Ephraim](/blog/author/nartey-ephraim/)

![Challenges facing teacher development in a school’s environment](https://res-4.cloudinary.com/adiutor-co/image/upload/q_auto/v1/ghost-blog-images/pexels-rdne-stock-project-7092613.jpg)

Teacher development is a cornerstone of educational progress, contributing to the growth and success of both educators and students. It encompasses a range of activities and opportunities designed to enhance teachers' knowledge, skills, and instructional practices, ultimately benefiting student learning outcomes. However, teacher development in the school environment faces various challenges that can hinder its effectiveness. This article delves into the concept of teacher development, explores the challenges educators encounter, and offers ways to help normalize teacher development in the school environment.

💡

**Lessons learnt:** While we teach, we learn.

### What is Teacher Development?

Teacher development refers to a continuous process of professional growth and learning aimed at improving teaching practices, pedagogical strategies, and subject knowledge. It involves a range of activities, including workshops, training programs, mentoring, collaborative lesson planning, and reflective practices. According to Darling-Hammond (2017), teacher development encompasses the acquisition of new knowledge, skills, and dispositions that enable educators to enhance their instructional practices, create inclusive learning environments, and adapt to evolving educational needs.

### Why is teacher development important?

Research consistently highlights the positive impact of effective teacher development on students' academic competence. A meta-analysis conducted by Kraft and Papay (2014) demonstrated that teachers who engage in sustained professional development exhibit increased instructional effectiveness and positively impact student achievement. High-quality teacher development programs that focus on content knowledge, pedagogical strategies, and classroom management contribute to improved student engagement, learning outcomes, and overall academic performance (Desimone, 2009). Students taught by highly developed teachers demonstrate higher levels of critical thinking, problem-solving abilities, and social-emotional development (Garet et al., 2001). Investing in teacher development ultimately leads to better educational experiences and outcomes for students.

### Challenges Facing Teacher Development in Schools

Teacher development in schools faces several challenges that can impede its effectiveness. One significant challenge is the limited availability of time and resources dedicated to professional development. Teachers often face heavy workloads and time constraints that make it difficult to engage in sustained and meaningful professional learning (OECD, 2019). Research by Darling-Hammond (2017) emphasizes the need for adequate time and resources to support teacher development and the importance of valuing professional learning as an essential part of teachers' work.

Conflicting demands also pose challenges to teacher development. Teachers are often faced with numerous responsibilities, including lesson planning, grading, and administrative tasks, which can detract from their ability to engage fully in professional development activities. Balancing these competing demands requires careful attention to prioritizing professional learning within the school schedule (Ingersoll & Strong, 2011).

Another notable challenge is the lack of alignment between teacher development opportunities and the specific needs and context of individual schools and teachers. Professional development programs that are not tailored to address the unique challenges and goals of the school community may result in limited impact on teaching practices and student outcomes (Ingersoll & Strong, 2011). Customizing professional learning experiences to meet the specific needs of teachers and schools can enhance their relevance and effectiveness (Darling-Hammond, 2017).

Inadequate support systems have also been found to hinder teacher development. Teachers benefit from ongoing support and feedback to guide their professional growth. However, many schools lack the structures and resources necessary to provide teachers with effective coaching, mentoring, or collaborative opportunities (OECD, 2019). Building robust support systems within schools, including mentoring programs, instructional coaching, and professional learning communities, is essential for fostering teacher development (Knight, 2009).

### Ways to Boost Teacher Development in Schools

Providing dedicated time and resources easily accessible for professional learning and collaboration is essential. This can involve scheduling regular professional development days, creating opportunities for peer observation and feedback, and encouraging participation in workshops and conferences (Hargreaves, 2015). Taking advantage of educational technologies can help lessen the load on teachers and create openings in their schedules to utilize these resources.

Fostering a culture of continuous improvement through reflective practices, such as action research or lesson study, allows teachers to reflect on their practices, share insights, and refine their instructional approaches (Lieberman & Miller, 2011).

Establishing mentoring programs, instructional coaching, and professional learning communities can offer personalized support and guidance to teachers, facilitating their growth and development (Knight, 2009).

Teacher development plays a pivotal role in fostering effective teaching practices and student academic competence. Despite the challenges it faces, concerted efforts can be made to boost teacher development in schools. The benefits of teacher development extend beyond individual teachers, positively impacting students' learning experiences and outcomes.

--- SOURCE 3: Solving the 6 Professional Development Challenges - MobileMind ---
URL: https://www.mobilemind.io/solving-the-6-professional-development-challenges

SUMMARY:
In addition, the ability for a teacher to personalize his or her learning experience makes the learning process more efficient than one-size-fits-all training that might not make the best use of educators’ valuable time. Either way, teachers (like students) need different levels and training and varied content that meets their skills level and learning goals, and instructional leaders need accurate visibility into teacher progress. And, while the value of face-to-face learning has undisputed merits, one-off training is problematic when trying to provide teachers with up-to-date PD on rapidly changing technology like Google, LMSs, and any other popular edtech tools used. Synchronizing PD efforts brings teachers' professional learning full circle, and allows for a smooth transition from in-person PD events and asynchronous events or training.

FULL CONTENT:
Solving the 6 Professional Development Challenges

Although there is quite a bit of in-depth research regarding effective professional development (PD) and best practices for educators, studies suggest that typical PD neglects to adequately address the needs of these adult learners. Research on adult learning supports the fact that there is a disconnect between research-based best practices and the traditional PD models found in schools. Unfortunately, it is rather challenging to meet the needs of all adult learners in typical face-to-face professional learning. The good news is that, with a more modern, virtual approach to PD, we can tackle such challenges, offering innovative personalized solutions for teachers.



In studies of educators’ dissatisfaction with traditional professional development, as well as leaders’ struggles with planning for teacher training, several themes repeatedly emerge in the findings. Challenges with traditional PD for teachers include, but are not limited to:





Below we will address each challenge and discuss how to leverage personalized learning to meet the needs of all educator-learners.

Buy-In

In the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation study Teachers Know Best, teachers report wanting professional development that is relevant, sustainable, interactive, and teacher-driven. They want to be respected as professionals. Understandably, teachers wish for their knowledge and experience to be valued, as well as considered during the PD design process.



When teachers are required to sit through one-size-fits-all professional development, it is easy for them to become frustrated or even resentful. Unintentional PD design undoubtedly contributes to educators’ dissatisfaction with mandated teacher training, repeatedly leaving teachers with a bad taste in their mouths when it comes to any type of continued learning.



Adult learning theories such as Knowles’ Theory of Andragogy proposed concepts that most certainly initiated a revolution in the world of adult education. We know that educators (and all adults, for that matter) prefer professional development relevant to their needs and has practical use. Abundant research on adult learning has revealed the importance of offering learning opportunities that are problem-centered, self-directed, and lead to the immediate utility of newly acquired skills.



At MobileMind, we wholeheartedly believe that educators deserve high-quality, meaningful professional development that is grounded in such research. Providing PD grounded in said research is absolutely essential if any professional development initiative is to succeed. Our self-paced, personalized micro-courses allow teachers to guide their own learning.



Each teacher can engage in training relevant to his or her unique challenges, needs, and goals. We want teachers to know that their time is valued and for everyone to find success, whether they are seeking Google certifications or if they are brand new Google users wanting to build foundational skills. We ensure they reach success by offering micro-courses geared towards learners at any level. Finally, our hands-on challenges not only model the practicality of skills acquired but also allow educators to begin creating materials they can use immediately in their classrooms and schools.

Personalization

We often discuss personalizing the learning experience for students, but traditional professional development typically fails to personalize PD for our educators. Honestly, if you aren’t differentiating technology PD at scale for all your teachers, it’s a problem.



Teachers have told us countless times that a “one-size-fits-all” approach to technology training doesn’t work, and research has revealed this issue repeatedly when it comes to traditional PD offerings. If we train all teachers, leaders, and staff on the same content and in the same manner, we are marginalizing the majority of our adult learners and losing their interest from the very start, leading to failed technology adoption.



If we want teacher buy-in, as discussed above, we must provide differentiated learning experiences for teachers, just as we strive to do for our students. So how do you effectively offer targeted technology PD based on varying proficiency levels?



First, every single MobileMind micro-course is differentiated between beginner, intermediate, and advanced. Most of the time educators will fall into one of these three groups. So, it is very easy for a teacher to start learning based on their relative knowledge of a Google tool. Educators can rate their skill levels across Google tools, and their micro-course library will be tailored to meet their needs.



Additionally, we have certification pathways that are also segmented based on proficiency. For example, a brand new user to Google may need to complete some early foundational elements before they even think about things like Google Certification levels. They could start with our Google Basics learning path. You may also have a veteran Google user wishing to earn Google Educator certification - or even Google Trainer certification. We can help with that as well! We empower educators to choose their own learning pathways based on their knowledge and skills, as well as their professional goals.



Finally, we even offer micro-courses specific to leaders and staff. It’s great for an instructional leader to know what to look for in the classroom, for instance, but how can leaders leverage Google tools to improve school culture and productivity? We’ve got you covered there, too!

Sustainability ﻿

The pace of change in educational technology can make it seem nearly impossible for teachers and edtech leaders to stay current on updates and new features. Consider the constant evolution of Google tools, for example. With frequent updates to key Google tools, such as Google Drive and Classroom, there are simply not enough traditional training days to keep educators abreast of this dynamic suite of tools.



Many districts provided Google Classroom training during preplanning, for instance, only to watch Classroom undergo some pretty significant updates a month or so later. We’ve also seen schools receive Chromebooks or launch Google Workspace for Education in August or September, hoping that they can start using the tools before their first dedicated PD day a month or two later. When districts rely only on face-to-face training, educators are at the mercy of the district's schedule, and it might be weeks or even months before teachers can receive initial training or refresh training on tools they’re expected to be used in the classroom.



This helps explain the major shift to the concept of “always-on”, continuous learning. Educators need to have access to PD that they can hop into at any time and from anywhere. In addition, they need to up-to-date training on the tools they’re expected to be using regularly. This means offering job-embedded, on-demand PD to our extremely busy educators that is regularly updated.



When developing the MobileMind professional learning hub, we kept in mind the need to be able to update our content quickly based on the fact that technology is constantly evolving. So, it is our job is to stay current with all Google applications and share updates with educators so you don’t have to. Because we embrace a micro-learning methodology, we can shift very quickly when Google enhances their tools, meaning we can update a course quickly for our teachers.



Lastly, because we have made MobileMind easily accessible and available anytime, we are accomplishing our goal of facilitating meaningful job-embedded learning that is accommodating to teachers. MobileMind data shows that on average our learners access at the highest volume during work hours. This means that our Google PD is valuable and time conscious enough that teachers are choosing the right time and place to learn for them, individually. This can often also mean that they don’t have to learn after school hours following a long day of teaching. (We have facilitated quite a bit of after-school PD, and we all know that it is less than ideal!)



We know that educators have so much on their plates, so to have anytime, anywhere access to sustainable training at the click of a button is a win!

Time

There are a few challenging elements when it comes to the issue of time and facilitating effective PD.



First, we struggle to find time to offer effective teacher training during the school day, week, and even the school year. Not only does it consume quite a bit of time for the preparation itself, but you often have to remove teachers from classroom instruction and get subs - or have them dig into their own time for off-contract or after-hours PD.



Second, we must consider new information we now
have regarding the impact of time in relation to actual learning. What do we mean by that? Studies have shown that our attention span has declined significantly and steadily since the year 2000, or since the internet and digital tools started to become mainstream. Several resources report these days that our attention span is less than that of a goldfish. As educators ourselves, we’ve sat through hour-long and sometimes even day-long PD sessions that failed to meet our needs. It is truly easy to become very resentful when you feel that your time is not valued; we have felt this and have found through a great deal of research that we are not alone! It is imperative that we acknowledge how much teachers already have on their plates and then act accordingly when designing educator training.



So, how has our team at MobileMind addressed the challenge of time?



At MobileMind, we embrace micro-learning and personalization. We have created what we call “micro-courses”, which are comprised of very brief learning videos (never longer than 5 minutes!) supported by short, hands-on learning challenges. In addition, the ability for a teacher to personalize his or her learning experience makes the learning process more efficient than one-size-fits-all training that might not make the best use of educators’ valuable time.



Teachers can begin learning at the click of a (Chrome extension) button. They can filter their personal micro-course library by their own learning goals. They can stop and then continue learning at any time to fit their busy schedules. Finally, they receive human feedback for each challenge submission, which further demonstrates how much we value each learner’s time and energy. Within seconds, a teacher can click to begin learning something new… no subs needed, no more dreaded after-school training, no more time wasted.

Limited Resources

We have heard from countless instructional leaders that the task of training teachers spread out over many school buildings can be daunting. The combination of multiple physical locations and the distance between them increases the difficulty of facilitating sustainable training.



On top of this, in many schools and districts, there is typically a small number of dedicated resources for training. This varies by district, but we see that the number of instructional resources relative to the number of teachers is rarely a ratio that enables easy facilitation of PD for technology like Google. Finally, there is very little visibility that instructional leaders can tap into regarding how well teachers are doing after traditional PD is offered. Do they need more help? Are they superstars who need a greater challenge? Either way, teachers (like students) need different levels and training and varied content that meets their skills level and learning goals, and instructional leaders need accurate visibility into teacher progress.



MobileMind is cloud-based, accessible by a browser extension. Anytime, Anywhere access to professional learning removes the physical barriers of training. And, while the value of face-to-face learning has undisputed merits, one-off training is problematic when trying to provide teachers with up-to-date PD on rapidly changing technology like Google, LMSs, and any other popular edtech tools used.



At MobileMind we offer virtual, personalized PD, so we can establish multiple schools or groups who can learn anytime and anywhere… during planning when they find 10 minutes or in their PJs at home... whenever works best for each individual teacher!



What’s more, is that district or school leaders have a bird's eye view of teacher progress in MobileMind and how many teachers at each school are engaging in PD. They can even drill down into any group to see how active each teacher is at a particular school has been in MobileMind.



Not only does this provide visibility for accountability, but leaders also can gain insights into training needs, teacher interest, struggling teachers, or previously unidentified teacher leaders. We can build up those teacher rockstars and begin to accelerate adoption and build internal capacity much more rapidly.



MobileMind’s cloud-based, location management gives us the ability to facilitate sustainable and effective PD at scale, regardless of logistical challenges, while expanding the reach of instructional resources and providing actionable insights to assist teacher progress.

Consolidation

There has never been an edtech solution that has truly integrated both synchronous and asynchronous professional learning efforts. This can make tracking continuing education hours and offering a cohesive professional learning experience impossible for districts that value continuous learning.



MobileMind 
Events provides the only synchronized PD solution to K-12 school districts. From workshops to conferences, MobileMind Sync allows leaders to easily create and schedule synchronous training events, invite participants, track attendance, and more.



Districts can even assign prerequisite courses to learners before a synchronous event, share related courses to complement live training, or offer replacement courses if learners cannot attend.



Sync join codes make it possible for facilitators to automate tasks like taking attendance, awarding credit hours, releasing digital badges after an event, and much more.



Synchronizing PD efforts brings teachers' professional learning full circle, and allows for a smooth transition from in-person PD events and asynchronous events or training.

# Navigating Professional Development Challenges with Strobel Education

![Kim teaching workshop - professional development challenges concept](https://strobeleducation.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/Kim-teaching-workshop-1024x680.jpg)

Professional development is a cornerstone of educational excellence, yet educators often grapple with significant obstacles that hinder their growth and effectiveness.

From the tightrope walk of juggling professional learning with teaching responsibilities to the scarcity of resources and personalized support, these challenges can seem insurmountable. However, with the right strategies and a supportive community, educators can transform these hurdles into stepping stones for success.

In this blog post, we’ll explore pragmatic solutions to the common professional development hurdles facing educators today. Drawing on the expertise of Strobel Education, we’ll delve into actionable advice for managing time, accessing resources, receiving tailored support, and embracing innovative teaching methods—all while prioritizing teacher well-being and student achievement.

Join us as we navigate the path to a more enriching and impactful teaching experience, and see how overcoming these challenges can lead to a thriving educational environment for all.

## Addressing Common Professional Development Challenges for Educators

Educators frequently encounter the obstacle of time constraints, as they strive to balance the demands of professional development (PD) with their primary teaching responsibilities. This struggle can be compounded by the limited time available for pre-planning and grading, which can often take precedence over PD due to immediate classroom needs.

Below are some common challenges faced by schools and educators when it comes to professional development. Overcoming these obstacles requires [strategic efforts](https://strobeleducation.com/blog/effective-teacher-professional-development-strategies/) and flexibility to foster an environment that values growth, innovation, and ongoing learning.

![Professional Development Challenges for Educators](https://strobeleducation.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/Professional-Development-Challenges-for-Educators-Desktop.jpg)
![Professional Development Challenges for Educators](https://strobeleducation.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/Professional-Development-Challenges-for-Educators-Mobile.jpg)

### Initial Buy-In

The challenge of buy-in from teachers, principals, and school districts can be significant in implementing effective professional development initiatives. To mitigate this, PD programs should be designed to be relevant, engaging, and directly applicable to the classroom, helping staff to see the immediate benefits to their teaching practice and student learning outcomes.

It’s essential to communicate the value and potential impact of professional development clearly, highlighting how it aligns with school goals and supports educators in addressing classroom problems. Building a collaborative approach that involves input from all stakeholders can help foster buy-in and create a shared vision for the importance of ongoing professional growth.

### Limited Resources

Limited resources can pose a significant challenge for schools looking to implement effective professional development programs. A lack of funding may restrict access to high-quality training materials, expert facilitators, and technology resources. Moreover, the limited availability of resources and materials can hinder educators’ ability to engage in effective learning experiences, leaving them without the necessary tools to implement new strategies or improve their skills.

Ensuring administrative support and adequate resources is crucial for the success of any PD program. School leaders should allocate funds and resources to support high-quality professional development, demonstrating its value to teaching staff.

Schools can also explore partnerships with local educational organizations, businesses, or community groups to access resources at reduced or no cost. Additionally, leveraging open educational resources and online platforms can provide cost-effective alternatives for delivering professional development content.

Collaborating with other schools or districts to share resources and expertise can also expand the reach of professional development initiatives while minimizing individual resource constraints. By collaborating to compile and share teaching aids, lesson plans, and professional literature, educators can create a dynamic pool of knowledge.

Additionally, actively seeking grants and funding opportunities can help acquire advanced resources that might otherwise be inaccessible. Leveraging connections with alumni networks and industry partners can also yield valuable expertise and materials, further enriching the spectrum of tools available for educator growth.

### Resistance to Change

Resistance to change can be a significant hurdle, whether from within themselves or from colleagues, when introduced to new teaching methodologies. This resistance can stem from a variety of factors, including comfort with established practices, skepticism about new approaches, or a lack of understanding of their potential benefits.

[Fostering a growth mindset](https://strobeleducation.com/blog/developing-a-growth-mindset-for-success/) and resilience among educators is vital to embracing change and innovation in teaching. By cultivating an attitude that views challenges as opportunities for development, educators can more readily adapt to new teaching methodologies. Teachers who believe in their ability to grow are more likely to engage in innovative practices and contribute positively to a dynamic educational environment.

Showcasing success stories and case studies of innovative teaching strategies can inspire educators to explore new approaches with confidence. When they see real-world examples of how change leads to improved student engagement and educational outcomes, teachers may become more open to experimentation. Encouraging educators to take calculated risks in a supportive environment can lead to discovery of practices that resonate with their teaching style and meet the diverse needs of their students.

### Time Constraints and Scheduling

Educators often grapple with finding enough hours in the day to juggle teaching, planning, grading, and their own professional development (PD). Time constraints and scheduling conflicts are frequent issues, often making it difficult for teachers to engage in PD activities without disrupting their primary responsibilities.

To navigate these time constraints, it’s crucial to identify PD activities that make the greatest impact on teaching effectiveness. Teachers can explore flexible PD options like online courses and webinars that fit into their schedules outside of school hours.

[Virtual learning](https://strobeleducation.com/blog/virtual-learning-for-school-teachers/) can also be an effective solution to scheduling conflicts. By offering professional development in a virtual format, educators can engage in learning activities without the need to travel to a physical location or disrupt their regular teaching schedule. They provide the flexibility for teachers to participate from their own homes or classrooms, making it easier to accommodate their busy schedules and varied time zones. Additionally, recorded sessions can be accessed later, allowing educators to engage with the material at a time that suits them best.

Educators and administrators working together can design a PD calendar that aligns with the school year’s rhythm, ensuring that professional learning does not overwhelm teaching responsibilities. Schools can offer PD opportunities during in-service days, as part of collaborative planning periods, or through [online modules that can be completed at the teacher’s convenience](https://strobeleducation.com/blog/flexible-learning-self-paced-professional-development-courses/). This integration helps to create a seamless blend of professional growth and classroom application, maximizing the value of every moment spent on PD.

##### [Click Here to see Strobel Education’s Upcoming Virtual Workshops](https://strobeleducation.com/teacher-workshops/)

Invest in your career with our comprehensive list of ive virtual workshops designed to address a specific area of teaching and learning.

## Tying Professional Development to Student Success

![Male teacher sitting with students and smiling](https://strobeleducation.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/male-secondary-or-high-school-teacher-sitting-at-t-2023-12-06-03-01-13-utc-1024x683.jpg)

Educators frequently embark on professional development (PD) with the ultimate goal of improving student outcomes. Therefore, aligning PD objectives with student learning outcomes is crucial to ensure that the skills and knowledge gained by teachers translate directly into an [enriched professional development](https://strobeleducation.com/blog/enriched-professional-development/) experience, and ultimately, an enhanced classroom experiences.

When PD is closely tied to the [goals of student success](https://strobeleducation.com/blog/prioritize-student-achievement/), educators can more effectively implement strategies that resonate with their students, leading to more meaningful learning experiences and higher levels of student engagement.

Involving students in the PD feedback process can be a transformative approach. By seeking input from students about teaching methods and classroom dynamics, educators gain unique insights that can inform their professional growth.

Additionally, measuring the impact of PD on classroom effectiveness and student engagement offers a tangible way to assess whether the PD is delivering the desired results. This measurement can take the form of a student survey or observation by peers who have been trained in evaluating classroom dynamics.

When [PD is closely linked to positive outcomes for students](https://journal.imse.com/teacher-pd-and-student-achievement/), educators are motivated and inspired to continue collaborating with colleagues on professional growth initiatives.

## The Role of Strobel Education in Overcoming PD Challenges

![Kim Strobel at head of education workshop class](https://strobeleducation.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/Kin-at-head-of-workshop-1024x683.jpg)

Strobel Education plays a pivotal role in addressing and surmounting the hurdles educators face during their professional development journey. By offering a diverse range of professional development opportunities such as teacher workshops, [online courses](https://strobeleducation.com/online-courses/) and [on-site training](https://strobeleducation.com/onsite-training/), Strobel Education meticulously designs experiences that directly tackle the common impediments to educator growth.

These PD opportunities provide actionable strategies and are tailored to fit the pressing needs of teachers, ensuring that time spent in PD is both efficient and impactful. Furthermore, Strobel offers flexible learning solutions, allowing educators to participate in PD without the constraints of traditional scheduling, thus respecting their time and existing obligations.

Educators who engage with Strobel Education’s professional development resources frequently [share their success stories](https://strobeleducation.com/praise/), illustrating how they’ve implemented new strategies and witnessed tangible improvements in their teaching and student engagement. The testimonials of these educators serve as a beacon for others seeking to enhance their practice and overcome similar challenges.

## Take the Next Step Toward Educational Excellence

As we’ve explored throughout this post, professional development is essential for educators who are committed to both their growth and the success of their students. However, educators often encounter significant roadblocks in their PD journey. Teachers may feel overwhelmed with their existing workload and cautious about investing time in new PD programs. Principals and school districts may face budget constraints or competing priorities, making it challenging to allocate resources to PD.

At [Strobel Education](https://strobeleducation.com/), we understand these challenges intimately and have crafted our teacher workshops, online courses, and customized PD experiences to help you navigate them with ease.

With our support, you can integrate professional development into your busy schedule, access a wealth of resources tailored to your needs, and embrace change with confidence. Let us help you strengthen your well-being and enhance your teaching strategies, ensuring that every step you take in your professional journey also benefits your students.

[Reach out to Strobel Education today](https://strobeleducation.com/contact/), and together, we can transform the challenges of professional development into opportunities for growth and student-centered learning.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Teachers often struggle to balance their demanding workload with professional development, leading to feelings of overwhelm and resentment towards PD initiatives.
Many schools face budget constraints that limit access to quality PD resources, making it difficult to provide meaningful training and support for educators.
Low engagement in PD sessions can stem from irrelevant content and a lack of interactive learning, leading to disinterest and ineffective professional growth.
Sustaining effective PD programs requires ongoing support and resources, yet many initiatives lack follow-up and adaptability to meet evolving educational needs.
